---
title: "Functional connectivity"
subtitle: "Seed-to-target correlation and seed-regressor GLM on the typed fmrimod substrate."
jupyter: python3
execute:
  freeze: auto
---

Functional connectivity is not a core fmrimod modeling seam, but the common
seed-to-target operation should not force every user to rewrite Pearson
correlation by hand. fmrimod gives you the typed substrate the analysis should
sit on: a dataset that owns its timing, a **typed nuisance model** instead of a
hand-built matrix, projection through the same `residualize` used by the
first-level path, a compact seed-correlation primitive, and the same `fmri_lm`
machinery when you want the seed signal as a GLM covariate.

## A Dataset, Not Bare Arrays

Start from an `fmri_dataset`. It carries the data, the TR, and the sampling
frame together, so every downstream step reads its timing from one typed
source instead of re-deriving it.

```{python}
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import fmrimod as fm
from fmrimod import baseline_model, design_matrix
from fmrimod.residualize import residualize
from fmrimod.stats import seed_target_correlation, seed_target_lm

rng = np.random.default_rng(13)
n_time, n_vox = 160, 9

# Voxel 0 is the seed; voxels 1-3 are genuinely coupled to it.
data = rng.normal(size=(n_time, n_vox))
data[:, 1:4] += 0.45 * data[:, 0:1]
motion = rng.normal(size=(n_time, 6))   # e.g. realignment parameters

dataset = fm.fmri_dataset(data, tr=2.0)
dataset.n_timepoints, dataset.n_voxels
```

## A Typed Nuisance Model

The drift, intercept, and motion confounds are a **typed `baseline_model`**,
not `np.column_stack([...])`. The model owns the column structure; you never
hand-assemble the nuisance matrix.

```{python}
bmodel = baseline_model(
    basis="poly",
    degree=2,
    sframe=dataset.sampling_frame,
    intercept="runwise",
    nuisance_list=[motion],
)

design_matrix(bmodel).shape   # (time, drift + intercept + motion columns)
```

## Residualize Through the Typed Path

`residualize` dispatches on the typed model — pass `bmodel` itself, not its
matrix. This is the same projection the first-level seam uses, so the cleaned
signal is consistent with how fmrimod fits everything else.

```{python}
clean = residualize(bmodel, dataset.get_all_data())
clean.shape
```

## Seed-to-Target Correlation

For a cleaned ROI matrix, the effect-size estimate is one explicit call. The
helper preserves the readable "seed versus target columns" shape and adds
Fisher-*z* columns for group-ready effect sizes.

```{python}
seed_target_correlation(
    clean,
    seed=0,
    target_names=[f"roi_{j + 1}" for j in range(clean.shape[1] - 1)],
).round(3)
```

## Seed as a GLM Covariate

The fmrireg-style inferential version treats the seed time series as a sampled
BOLD covariate. That path is a thin wrapper over `fmri_lm`: it fits each target
on `[intercept, confounds, seed]` and reports the seed coefficient, standard
error, *t*, and *p*.

```{python}
seed_target_lm(
    dataset.get_all_data(),
    seed=0,
    confounds=design_matrix(bmodel),
    add_intercept=False,
    target_names=[f"roi_{j + 1}" for j in range(clean.shape[1] - 1)],
).to_frame().round(3)
```

## Back to fmrimod for Group-Ready Effect Sizes

`seed_target_correlation` already includes the Fisher *z* and sampling variance
`1 / (n - 3)`, which is exactly what the group seam (`fmri_meta`, `group_fit`)
expects per connection. The lower-level `r_to_z` helper remains available when
you already have correlations from another source.

```{python}
fc = seed_target_correlation(clean, seed=0)
z = fc["fisher_z"].to_numpy()
z_var = fc["z_variance"].to_numpy()
pooled_r = fm.z_to_r(z.mean())

pd.DataFrame({
    "fisher_z": z,
    "z_variance": z_var,
}).round(3)
```

```{python}
round(float(z.mean()), 3), round(float(pooled_r), 3)
```

The coupled targets (`roi_1`–`roi_3`) carry the visible correlation; the
Fisher *z* and its variance are now in the shape the group-level tools
consume. Motion correction, registration, and normalization remain outside
fmrimod's scope — the substrate here begins at a preprocessed dataset.

## Next

- [Group analysis](group-analysis.qmd)
- [Datasets](datasets.qmd)
